reg-001.zusammenfassung: Since 2024, a popular initiative (Hamburg Werbefrei — Hamburg Ad-Free) has been underway in Hamburg to restrict outdoor advertising, in particular digital advertising screens. The Hamburg Constitutional Court (Hamburgisches Verfassungsgericht) declared the initiative legally admissible in September 2024.
reg-001.kernbefund: The Hamburg Constitutional Court ruled that the initiative "Hamburg Werbefrei" (Hamburg Ad-Free) is constitutionally permissible. Around 400 activists collected over 50,000 signatures in 3 weeks (target: 80,000 = 5% of eligible voters, required for a binding referendum — Volksentscheid). The initiative calls for a ban on digital advertising screens and a reduction in poster advertising on grounds of environmental, public health, and urban landscape protection.
reg-001.relevanz: The Hamburg initiative demonstrates that there is broad public support for restrictions on outdoor advertising. When a major German city holds a democratic vote on advertising bans, it sends a powerful signal that society recognises the harms caused by outdoor advertising.

reg-002.zusammenfassung: Germany was the last EU member state to ban outdoor tobacco advertising. The ban was introduced in stages: tobacco products from January 2022, heated tobacco products from January 2023, and e-cigarettes from January 2024.
reg-002.kernbefund: The tobacco industry spent approximately 100 million euros per year on street and cinema advertising in Germany. For decades Germany was a special case within the EU: while other member states had long since banned outdoor tobacco advertising, Germany's politically influential tobacco industry blocked the prohibition. The ban only came following significant public and EU pressure.
reg-002.relevanz: This example illustrates how the advertising industry can delay legislative safeguards for decades, even when the harm — tobacco advertising leading to youth smoking — has long been scientifically established. It sets a precedent for other harmful advertising in public spaces.

reg-003.zusammenfassung: São Paulo introduced the "Lei Cidade Limpa" (Clean City Law) in 2007 — the most comprehensive outdoor advertising ban in the world. 15,000 billboards were removed, digital screens banned, advertising on buses prohibited, and 300,000 non-compliant business signs taken down.
reg-003.kernbefund: A survey in 2011 (4 years after the ban) showed that 70% of São Paulo's 11 million residents regarded the ban positively. Unexpected consequences: the ban revealed historic architecture that had previously been obscured by advertising, and it stimulated street art. Clear Channel challenged the law in court — and lost.
reg-003.relevanz: São Paulo is proof that life without outdoor advertising in public spaces is possible — and is welcomed by the majority of the population. It refutes the advertising industry's argument that outdoor advertising is an indispensable part of city life.

reg-004.zusammenfassung: Grenoble (France) became the first European city to introduce a comprehensive outdoor advertising ban in its urban area in 2015. Mayor Éric Piolle (Greens) terminated the city's billboard contract and had 326 advertising panels (including 64 large billboards) removed.
reg-004.kernbefund: 2,051 square metres of advertising space were eliminated. As a replacement: 300+ new community notice boards (Bürgertafeln) for non-profit announcements, trees, and open spaces for local cultural groups. Financial loss: 600,000 euros per year in advertising revenue. The model demonstrates that cities can forgo advertising revenue and reclaim public space.
reg-004.relevanz: Grenoble proves that it is politically and financially feasible to eliminate outdoor advertising in a European city. The city is now a reference case for urban ad-free movements worldwide.

reg-005.zusammenfassung: Analysis by the European consumer organisation BEUC (Bureau Européen des Unions de Consommateurs) of the EU Pledge — the food industry's voluntary commitment not to advertise unhealthy products to children. Conclusion: total failure of self-regulation.
reg-005.kernbefund: 21 companies responsible for over 80% of European food advertising expenditure have signed up to the EU Pledge. Nevertheless, 85% of the products these companies market to children would be prohibited from advertising under WHO nutritional criteria. Voluntary self-regulatory commitments are ineffective.
reg-005.relevanz: Outdoor advertising in Germany and the EU is also governed primarily by self-regulation (Deutscher Werberat — German Advertising Standards Council). This example demonstrates systemically that voluntary industry codes do not protect children. Statutory prohibitions are necessary.

reg-006.zusammenfassung: Academic analysis of the structural weaknesses of German advertising self-regulation (Deutscher Werberat — German Advertising Standards Council). Documents structural deficiencies that prevent effective consumer protection.
reg-006.kernbefund: The Deutscher Werberat (German Advertising Standards Council) has no consumer representation on its board — only a liaison committee. It can demand voluntary compliance but has no binding enforcement powers. Advertising practices can continue unimpeded while proceedings are in progress. In 2023 the Council issued only 8 formal reprimands (Rügen) despite thousands of advertisements in circulation.
reg-006.relevanz: Outdoor advertising in Germany is in practice subject to no effective substantive regulation. The Werberat is the principal protective instrument — and it does not protect. This leaves harmful advertising in public spaces structurally unregulated.

reg-007.zusammenfassung: In 2021, §41a was inserted into the Bundesnaturschutzgesetz (BNatSchG — Federal Nature Conservation Act), providing for protection against light emissions. The provision is not yet in force — it requires an implementing ordinance (Durchführungsverordnung) announced for 2027 as part of the national biodiversity strategy.
reg-007.kernbefund: The German legislature has officially recognised light pollution as a nature conservation problem and incorporated it into the Federal Nature Conservation Act. Illuminated advertising panels and digital DOOH screens in Germany are in urban areas almost twice as numerous as street lamps, and contribute substantially to light pollution.
reg-007.relevanz: If §41a BNatSchG comes into force in 2027, it could for the first time provide a statutory basis for regulating advertising illumination as a source of light emissions. At present, specific statutory rules for outdoor advertising illumination in Germany are lacking.

reg-008.zusammenfassung: In several large cities in North Rhine-Westphalia (NRW) — particularly Cologne (Köln), the home city of Ströer, and Münster — civil society initiatives have formed that are calling for a ban or strong restrictions on commercial outdoor advertising.
reg-008.kernbefund: These initiatives aim to amend local building regulations (Bauordnung) so that digital advertising installations (DOOH) and alternating illuminated signs in public spaces are prohibited. They argue on grounds of visual noise (visueller Lärm), unnecessary energy consumption during the climate crisis, and the commercialisation of cities.
reg-008.relevanz: Resistance to outdoor advertising is coalescing strongly at grassroots level directly within the municipalities where the advertising structures are installed. This reflects the growing discontent of civil society with the privatisation of public space.

reg-009.zusammenfassung: Advertising corporations exploit gaps in municipal building and planning regulations to digitalise public spaces without renewed public participation.
reg-009.kernbefund: In many municipalities, contracts or permits exist for existing "illuminated advertising installations" (e.g. analogue City Light Posters). Outdoor advertisers are increasingly using these existing permits to physically replace the paper poster with a bright DOOH LED screen ("digital upgrade"). Because the physical dimensions of the casing often remain the same, planning authorities frequently — and incorrectly — do not classify this as a new construction, thereby bypassing environmental assessments and objections from local residents.
reg-009.relevanz: The stark differences in light emission and psychological impact between an analogue poster and a digital screen are being ignored through legal technicalities. A rigorous update of building regulations is urgently needed.

reg-010.zusammenfassung: The installation and operation of digital billboards frequently violates local building regulations. Corporations exploit the overloaded capacity of municipal authorities.
reg-010.kernbefund: Investigations in the United Kingdom reveal systematic disregard for conditions and requirements. DOOH installations are often erected entirely without planning permission. Even where conditions exist, contractually agreed brightness limits (luminance) are exceeded, or mandatory night-time switch-offs (e.g. from 23:00 to 06:00) are simply ignored. Advertising corporations often accept fines as a "cost of doing business", since revenue from the illegal advertising airtime is higher, and municipal planning departments are too understaffed to pursue Discontinuance Notices through the courts.
reg-010.relevanz: The industry cannot be effectively controlled. Once the hardware is physically installed, the burden of proof (measuring brightness) and the effort of legal enforcement rests entirely with municipalities and local residents.

reg-011.zusammenfassung: A growing number of municipal and regional governments are using their role as landowners to ban advertising for high-emission products on civic infrastructure.
reg-011.kernbefund: Cities including Edinburgh, Sheffield, Cambridgeshire, and Somerset have introduced legally binding clauses into their advertising policies. These prohibit advertising corporations (such as JCDecaux or Clear Channel) from displaying advertising for flights, fossil-fuel-powered SUVs, and fossil fuels on municipal land (e.g. bus shelters, council-owned hoardings). The municipalities argue that promoting these products is in direct contradiction to their urban climate protection targets ("Net Zero").
reg-011.relevanz: These policy frameworks prove that regulating the *content* of outdoor advertising at the local level is legally feasible and politically supported. They place "high-carbon" products on the same prohibited footing as tobacco or weapons advertising.

reg-012.zusammenfassung: The legal assumption that a complete ban on outdoor advertising constitutes an impermissible interference with economic freedom (Wirtschaftsfreiheit) has been refuted by Switzerland's highest court.
reg-012.kernbefund: The municipality of Vernier (Canton of Geneva) resolved to ban commercial poster advertising throughout its territory in order to protect the visual landscape and reduce visual environmental pollution. Advertising corporations (including APG SGA) challenged the ban through every level of the court system. In 2024, the Swiss Federal Supreme Court (Bundesgericht) issued a final ruling in favour of the municipality: the ban is lawful. The court found that the public interest in an intact local environment and in protecting residents from unsolicited consumer pressure outweighs the purely commercial interests of the advertising industry.
reg-012.relevanz: This ruling is a massive legal precedent for the whole of Europe. It gives cities and municipalities the legal certainty that zero-billboard policies are proportionate and enforceable.

reg-013.zusammenfassung: Bodies of "voluntary self-regulation" (such as the Werberat — Advertising Standards Council — in Germany, or the ASA — Advertising Standards Authority — in the UK) are unable to effectively prevent harmful outdoor advertising.
reg-013.kernbefund: The advertising industry's self-regulatory systems share a structural design flaw: they operate almost exclusively reactively. There is no pre-clearance process for posters. This means that severely harmful or misleading advertising is first displayed and forced upon the public (including children). By the time complaints are submitted, reviewed, and formal reprimands issued, the typical one-to-two-week booking period for the poster has in any case already expired. The harm has already occurred.
reg-013.relevanz: "We comply with the rules of the Werberat" is an industry argument that implies safety but is in practice worthless. To protect citizens, cities must legally enshrine proactive exclusion criteria (such as London's junk food ban) into binding policy.

reg-014.zusammenfassung: A strict ban on outdoor advertising is not an economic disadvantage but a tangible economic competitive advantage — as four US states demonstrate.
reg-014.kernbefund: In the United States, the four states of Hawaii, Maine, Vermont, and Alaska have banned outdoor advertising across their entire territories by law. Contrary to the advertising industry's claim that bans harm local economies, the governments of these states argue the opposite: protecting the landscape ("Scenic Beauty") from visual pollution ("Billboard Blight") is a decisive factor in promoting tourism and quality of life. Preserving an unobstructed landscape is economically more valuable than advertising revenue.
reg-014.relevanz: These substantial legal precedents undermine the industry's "TINA" argument (There Is No Alternative). A complete ban on outdoor advertising is implementable at state level and economically sound.

reg-015.zusammenfassung: The French government has enacted landmark climate and advertising legislation at the national level that directly affects the business models of OOH (out-of-home) and DOOH (digital out-of-home) operators.
reg-015.kernbefund: The "Loi Climat et Résilience" (Climate and Resilience Act), which entered into force in August 2022, bans advertising for fossil fuels (gas, coal, oil) nationwide. Additionally, this law now formally grants mayors and local authorities ("Maires") executive policing powers (Pouvoir de Police — executive enforcement authority) to strictly restrict or completely remove digital and illuminated advertising panels within their municipal area by local decree. Previously, this decision-making authority often resided at uncertain intermediate national-regional levels.
reg-015.relevanz: This is one of the most powerful legislative interventions in the EU. Advertising for climate-damaging products is placed on the same footing as tobacco advertising, and decision-making authority over digital light pollution is formally returned to cities.

reg-016.zusammenfassung: Dutch cities are increasingly using their control over public space to restrict advertising for emissions-intensive products. Amsterdam voted in January 2026 to ban fossil and meat advertising in public space; Haarlem was an earlier pioneer on meat advertising in 2022.
reg-016.kernbefund: Amsterdam's city council approved an APV-based ban on advertising for meat and fossil or climate-damaging products in public space. The exact start date was not finally fixed in the cited reporting; city officials indicated that a reasonable transition period was needed. The supported claim is therefore that Amsterdam adopted/formalised the ban, not that it was already fully implemented at every site.
reg-016.relevanz: The case shows that municipalities increasingly steer advertising content on city-controlled or locally regulated surfaces according to climate and public-interest criteria. For concession holders this creates contract and transition risk: existing advertising categories can be politically excluded, subject to local ordinance design, phase-in periods and contract terms.

reg-017.zusammenfassung: French environmental law actively protects rural areas and smaller municipalities from the visual flooding of commercial advertising.
reg-017.kernbefund: As part of a comprehensive reform of advertising law, France enacted legislation in 2010 that has been fully applicable since July 2015: in all municipalities with fewer than 10,000 inhabitants (that are not part of a larger metropolitan area), large-format commercial outdoor billboard advertising is fundamentally prohibited. Permitted only are specific directional signs for local cultural activities, regional products, or essential services. Violations expose advertisers to daily fines.
reg-017.relevanz: The law demonstrates that preserving the aesthetic integrity and quality of life in smaller municipalities is a protectable public interest that takes precedence over the commercial exploitation interests of the advertising industry.

reg-018.zusammenfassung: Planning law gives cities the means to limit visual clutter (Clutter) and the associated degradation of quality of life in residential neighbourhoods.
reg-018.kernbefund: In Bristol (UK), the city council has tightened guidelines interpreting the concept of "amenity" (quality of life / livability) in planning law. The aim is to prevent digital advertising screens from being disproportionately concentrated in districts that already have lower environmental quality or in lower-income neighbourhoods. The city argues that an excessive density of DOOH installations degrades the appearance of a neighbourhood, creates light pollution for residents, and violates the right to an ad-free, peaceful public space.
reg-018.relevanz: This is an instrument for enforcing spatial equity (räumliche Gerechtigkeit). It challenges the industry practice of flooding low-income neighbourhoods with cheap advertising while affluent neighbourhoods remain "clean".

reg-019.zusammenfassung: The previous claim was too broad: Belgium did not enact a complete national fossil-fuel advertising ban including OOH/DOOH in 2023. The supported narrower fact is that the 2023-2027 RTBF public-broadcaster agreement bans commercial communications for fossil fuels, while broader Belgian fossil-ad bans were legislative initiatives.
reg-019.kernbefund: The RTBF agreement prohibits fossil-fuel commercial communications in the public-broadcasting context. A separate Belgian bill proposed a wider fossil-ad ban, but NGO documentation describes it as an ongoing proposal with scope limitations, not as a fully enacted national OOH ban. This claim must therefore not be used as evidence that all fossil advertising in Belgian public space is already illegal.
reg-019.relevanz: Belgium remains relevant as a regulatory signal, but not as a hard proof of a national outdoor advertising ban. For a stronger municipal OOH legal precedent use The Hague (`reg-032`); Belgium is better read as evidence of sectoral media-contract restrictions and parliamentary momentum.

reg-020.zusammenfassung: A Tagesspiegel interview with the JCDecaux CEO: the managing director of the world's largest outdoor advertising company openly admits that the corporation deliberately cultivates networks with political decision-makers in cities in order to secure advertising rights contracts. This is the structural foundation of the "street furniture" (Stadtmöbel) business model.
reg-020.kernbefund: JCDecaux's business model is explicitly based on building relationships with political decision-makers in municipalities who decide on the allocation of advertising rights. The CEO stated: *"We know how to deal with decision-makers."* JCDecaux holds a 15-year exclusive contract in Cologne (Köln) for 1,550 passenger shelters and 350 city information systems. In Berlin, JCDecaux/Wall held the dominant street furniture contract for decades. Karlsruhe also renewed its advertising rights contracts with Wall/JCDecaux.
reg-020.relevanz: The "street furniture" (Stadtmöbel) model is not a service offering — it is a political lobbying strategy: municipalities receive infrastructure (bus shelters, clocks, public toilets) and in return grant exclusive advertising rights for 10–20 years, giving the corporation de facto control over public space. No city council can pass advertising bans during that period without risking contractual penalties.

reg-021.zusammenfassung: The Hague (Den Haag, Netherlands, population 500,000) became — as of 1 January 2025 — the first city in the world with a statutory ban on OOH advertising for fossil fuel products: specifically petrol/diesel vehicles, flights, and cruises. The ban applies to all billboards and bus shelters in public space. Other EU cities are following.
reg-021.kernbefund: The Den Haag ban is **a direct OOH/DOOH prohibition** — it explicitly covers billboards and passenger shelters in public space. The city thus heeds the call of UN Secretary-General António Guterres, who demanded a global ban on advertising for fossil fuels. In response, the Dutch travel association ANVR announced legal action. Further cities that are discussing or have enacted similar bans include: Amsterdam, Edinburgh, Sydney, Zwolle, Tilburg, and Graz (Austria). Belgium enacted a national fossil fuel advertising ban (all media) in 2023.
reg-021.relevanz: The Hague demonstrates that OOH/DOOH advertising for specific product categories can be legally banned — despite existing advertising rights contracts with corporations. The model will spread across the EU. For DOOH investors: two of the most lucrative advertising categories (automotive and aviation) are already banned in The Hague — with a signal effect for other EU markets, including Germany.

reg-022.zusammenfassung: The Hamburg Regional Court (Landgericht Hamburg) banned Shell Deutschland by judgment on 9 August 2024 from advertising with the promise "CO2-neutral driving for 1.1 cents per litre." The Deutsche Umwelthilfe (German Environmental Aid, a German environmental NGO) had filed suit in 2022 after Shell failed to cease the practice despite a formal warning (Abmahnung). The court ruled: the advertising was "highly misleading for consumers."
reg-022.kernbefund: Shell promised CO2 neutrality in its petrol station and OOH advertising through offset projects (forest protection in Peru and Indonesia). The court (case no. 315 O 108/22) found: (1) the advertising was not transparent about the offset method; (2) forest protection projects cannot reliably offset CO2 persistence over centuries — forests can release stored CO2 within years or decades. The Deutsche Umwelthilfe had established that across 6 major energy corporations, **81% of Shell's advertisements constituted greenwashing**.
reg-022.relevanz: The ruling applies directly to OOH advertising: Shell petrol station advertising and outdoor campaigns were part of the impugned advertising measures. The ruling sets a precedent for Germany: vague "climate neutrality" promises in OOH campaigns are legally challengeable. DOOH network operators that carry greenwashing campaigns from energy corporations expose themselves — and their clients — to legal risks.

reg-023.zusammenfassung: The European Commission opened a formal state aid (Staatsbeihilfe) investigation in 2015 into two contracts between the City of Brussels and JCDecaux. Result in 2019: JCDecaux had continued operating advertising panels ("phantom billboards") after the contracts expired — without paying rent or taxes. This constitutes illegal state aid incompatible with the EU internal market. The EU General Court (Gericht der Europäischen Union, EuG) dismissed JCDecaux's appeal.
reg-023.kernbefund: JCDecaux used municipal infrastructure in Brussels beyond the contractually agreed period without paying any consideration. Competitors had to pay regular market prices for comparable advertising rights. The European Commission classified this as an impermissible competitive advantage. The EU General Court (case no. T-642/19) upheld the Commission decision and dismissed JCDecaux's action in full. Competitor Clear Channel Belgium had filed the original complaint.
reg-023.relevanz: The case documents a concrete contract and state-aid risk in the OOH market: where operators use public advertising infrastructure beyond agreed terms or on non-market conditions, EU state-aid recovery can follow. For municipalities it is a warning to control term, payment and removal obligations transparently; for investors it shows that apparently stable municipal advertising rights can be legally vulnerable.

reg-024.zusammenfassung: The Swiss Federal Supreme Court (Bundesgericht) confirmed in 2024 that the municipality of Vernier (38,000 inhabitants, Canton of Geneva) may ban all commercial advertising panels in public space — including on private land. The ban serves to combat "visual pollution" and grants citizens the right to withdraw from unwanted advertising. More than three quarters of the 172 advertising panels have been removed. Zurich (Zürich) and Berne (Bern) have announced similar bans. OOH companies in Switzerland reportedly face an "existential threat."
reg-024.kernbefund: The Federal Supreme Court did not classify the complete advertising ban as an anti-competitive intervention but as a legitimate municipal measure against "visual environmental pollution" (visuelle Umweltverschmutzung). In Switzerland it is now legally recognised: outdoor advertising in public space is a form of environmental pollution against which citizens have a right to protection. The precedent ruling applies to all Swiss municipalities. Zurich in particular targets digital screens due to their energy consumption and light pollution.
reg-024.relevanz: The Federal Supreme Court ruling is the strongest judicial confirmation worldwide that complete OOH bans are legally sustainable. The "domino effect" in Switzerland — Vernier → Zurich → Berne — shows that a single precedent ruling can trigger an avalanche. For DOOH investors counting on long-term revenue from public locations, the Swiss model is a direct precursor to European legal developments.

reg-025.zusammenfassung: Amsterdam passed a law in January 2026 (by 27:17 council votes) — the first statutory ban by a capital city on OOH advertising for fossil fuels, meat, flights, petrol/diesel vehicles, and gas heating — covering all public surfaces including trams, trains, buses, and digital screens. In force from 1 May 2026. A second phase bans, from April 2028, corporate image advertising by high-emission companies as well. The city council withstood intensive lobbying pressure from industry and the national government.
reg-025.kernbefund: Amsterdam goes beyond The Hague (fossil fuels only) by including meat and flights for the first time. The vote was passed despite a final lobbying offensive. The ban covers all DOOH networks in public space. The 2028 second phase explicitly targets "Corporate Image Advertising" by companies with high CO₂ emissions — a precedent for corporate brand communications. The city council described lobbying by industry and government as inappropriate interference in democratic decision-making processes.
reg-025.relevanz: Amsterdam is the first EU capital city with such a ban. Together with The Hague (2025), Belgium (2023), France (2022), and now Amsterdam (2026), a tightening regulatory web is forming across the EU. Two of the most valuable DOOH categories — automotive and food — are banned in Amsterdam. DOOH investments in EU capital cities carry a structural and accelerating regulatory risk that threatens the profitability of existing contracts.

reg-026.zusammenfassung: EU Directive 2024/825 ("Empowering Consumers for the Green Transition"), adopted on 27 March 2024, bans generic environmental claims in advertising without specific evidence on the same medium. Banned from September 2026 (national transposition required by March 2026): statements such as "CO2-neutral", "climate-friendly", "energy-efficient", "green", "biodegradable" without quantified substantiation. Also banned: climate neutrality claims based solely on CO₂ offset projects.
reg-026.kernbefund: The Directive applies to all advertising formats — including OOH and DOOH. Since billboards offer physically limited space, generic greenwashing claims on DOOH screens will in practice become impossible: a "CO2-neutral" claim on a digital display without detailed supporting evidence on the same medium will be EU-unlawful from 2026. The associated Green Claims Directive (more detailed regulation) was withdrawn by the Commission in 2025 — but Directive 2024/825 remains in force.
reg-026.relevanz: DOOH networks in Germany and the EU generate substantial revenue from campaigns by energy companies, automotive manufacturers, and airlines carrying greenwashing messages. From September 2026, these campaigns in their current form will be EU-unlawful. DOOH operators bear co-responsibility for disseminating misleading advertising on their infrastructure — the Hamburg Regional Court (Landgericht Hamburg) confirmed this in 2024 in the Shell case (reg-022).
